line: He didn't want a fuss. Said just put him in the ground next to your grandmother.
Dad, who is Eleanor Parish?
Where did you hear that name?
I found a letter. In Grandpa's watch. It's addressed to someone named Eleanor —
Sophie. Put it back. Put it back where you found it and don't mention that name again.
Dad. I need to know.
You don't.
His last words, Dad. What were Grandpa's last words?
He said... 'Tell her I'm sorry.'
Tell who?
He didn't say. He never said. But I always knew.
Her name was Eleanor Parish. She was a nurse. British. He met her in England before D-Day. And that's all I know. That's all he ever told me.
Tommy Bennett. My God. Haven't heard that name in... well, I suppose it's been since the reunion in '71.
Mr. Whitfield, did you know a nurse named Eleanor Parish?

Know her? Sweetheart, everyone knew about Eleanor. Those two — Tommy and Eleanor — they were the only beautiful thing in that whole ugly war. You'd see them walking along the cliffs after their shifts, and you'd forget for a moment that half of us wouldn't make it home.
What happened to them?
D-Day happened.

Your grandfather was a proud man. And a stubborn one. He couldn't write worth a damn — farm boy, you know. Hands made for plows, not pens. I used to write his letters home for him. His mother, his sister — they all got letters in my handwriting.
The night before D-Day, Tom asked Eleanor to marry him. She said yes. And the next morning, the boats left for Normandy. That was the last time he saw her.
When this is over — when I come back — I want to build you a house. A real house. With a porch and a kitchen big enough to dance in.
You're proposing to me with a kitchen?
I'm proposing to you with everything I've got.
Keep it. Bring it back to me. That's your promise.
I'm sorry, Private. The field hospital in Dorset took a direct hit. E. Parish is on the casualty list.
Dad. Eleanor is alive. She survived the war. She's been alive this whole time.
I know.
What?
He found out. About ten years ago. A buddy from the war sent him an article from some British veterans' magazine. Eleanor Parish — alive, living in England.
And he didn't go to her?
Your grandmother was still alive, Sophie. He loved your grandmother too. Not the same way — but he loved her. He couldn't just... leave. He couldn't break her heart to mend his own.
He carried that knowledge for ten years. Knowing she was out there. Knowing he couldn't go. That's what finally made him quiet, Sophie. Not the war. Not the grief. The knowing.
Can I help you, dear?
My name is Sophie Bennett. I'm — I'm Tom's granddaughter.
Oh my God. You have his eyes.
This isn't Tom's handwriting.
What?
I knew Tom's handwriting. It was terrible — big, crooked letters, like a child's. He was embarrassed by it. This — this is beautiful penmanship. But it's not Tom's.
Then who wrote it?
I think... I think I know this handwriting.
George. George Whitfield. He labeled every supply crate in the hospital. I'd know that copperplate anywhere.

The night before D-Day, Tom came to me. He was shaking. Not because of the invasion — because of what he wanted to say. He had a piece of paper in his hands, covered in crossed-out words. He'd been trying to write me a letter.
He was a carpenter's son from Pennsylvania. He could build anything with his hands. But words? Written words? They defeated him. He told me once, 'My hands know how to say things. My pen doesn't.'

So he asked George — dear George, who had the most beautiful handwriting in the regiment — to write it for him. Tom spoke. George wrote. Every word in this letter came from Tom's mouth. George just gave them shape on paper.
But what about the end? The part that's smudged — I could never read it.
This part — the last two lines — this IS Tom's handwriting. After George finished, Tom took the pen and added something himself.
I can't write pretty words. But I love you is all I know. — Tom
Eleanor... if I had come sooner... would you have wanted to talk to him? On the phone? Would you have wanted to hear his voice?
Wanted to? I've rehearsed what I would say to that man every night for fifty years.
I'm so sorry. He passed away three weeks ago.
Fifty years I waited. And I was three weeks too late.
Every night, I said goodnight to this photograph. I told myself he could hear me, wherever he was. I told myself it wasn't foolish.
It wasn't foolish.
No. I suppose it wasn't.
I'm here, Tom. I'm finally here.
I got your letter. It only took fifty years, but I got it. And that last line — the one in your terrible, beautiful handwriting — that's the finest thing anyone has ever written to me.
You owe me a kitchen, Thomas Bennett. A kitchen big enough to dance in.
I wrote this on June 5th, 1944. The same night he wrote his.
I was going to give it to him when he came back. He never came back. So I kept it.
